1: “After these acts of faithfulness, Sennacherib king of Assyria came and invaded Judah.” The sequence is deliberate: covenant faithfulness does not remove trial — it determines how the trial is faced and resolved. As a theme, this pattern runs from Abraham (tested after his call) through Job, through the wilderness generation, and reaches its definitive expression in Christ’s temptation immediately after his baptismal commissioning (Matthew 4:1). The testing of the faithful is not an exception to God’s care but an instrument of it.
2: Hezekiah sees Sennacherib’s intention and takes action. The perception of threat and the measured response is the responsible exercise of human agency within divine sovereignty. As a theme, the wise king who correctly discerns the enemy’s intention anticipates Christ, who “knew what was in man” (John 2:25) and who sees through every form of opposition to its spiritual root.
3: Hezekiah consults his officials about stopping water supplies before the siege. As a theme, the prudent use of counsel and practical wisdom is not at odds with faith in YHWH — it is the embodiment of it. The NT wisdom tradition (James 1:5) calls for the same: asking God for wisdom and then acting wisely in the world. Christ perfectly unites divine wisdom with practical action (1 Corinthians 1:24: “Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God”).
4: The stopping of the water sources denies the enemy a vital resource. As a theme, the identification and strategic withdrawal of what sustains life from the enemy’s reach anticipates the NT warfare language: “Do not give the devil a foothold” (Ephesians 4:27). The spiritual warfare of the new covenant is fought with wisdom and preparation, grounded in the power of Christ.
5: Hezekiah rebuilds the broken walls and strengthens the city’s defenses. As a shadow, the faithful king repairing the breach in the city of God anticipates Nehemiah’s wall-building as an image of restoration. More directly, it points to Christ as the one who repairs the ultimate breach — the broken relationship between humanity and God — restoring the wall of salvation: “You shall call your walls Salvation and your gates Praise” (Isaiah 60:18).
6: Hezekiah assembles the commanders in the city gate square and speaks to the people. As a type, the king’s pre-battle address in the city square echoes the prophetic-royal preaching pattern. Christ’s own pre-passion addresses to his disciples (John 14-17) are the fulfillment of this pattern: the king speaking courage and divine assurance to his people before the ultimate battle.
7: “Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid or dismayed before the king of Assyria and all the horde with him, for there is one greater with us than with him.” The Deuteronomic formula of courage (ḥizqû wĕʾimṣû) was given first to Joshua and is now echoed by Hezekiah. As a type, the king who speaks courage to God’s people before the overwhelming enemy prefigures Christ’s words before his passion: “In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world” (John 16:33). “There is one greater with us” is the Emmanuel declaration in military form.
8: “With him is an arm of flesh, but with us is the LORD our God to help us and to fight our battles.” This is the Emmanuel principle in its starkest form: human military power (“arm of flesh”) versus divine presence. As a direct type of Christ, this verse establishes the contrast that Matthew announces in naming Jesus “Emmanuel (which means, God with us)” (Matthew 1:23). The LORD who fights for his people in 2 Chr 32 is the same God who, in Christ, fights and wins the ultimate battle against sin, death, and the devil — not with an arm of flesh but with the cross.
9: Sennacherib sends envoys to undermine Hezekiah’s position while he himself remains at Lachish. As a theme, the enemy attacking the covenant people through representatives while the ruler directs the assault from a distance echoes the NT’s portrait of spiritual warfare: the adversary “prowls around like a roaring lion” (1 Peter 5:8), operating through human agents to undermine faith in God’s people.
10: Sennacherib’s taunting question — “On what are you relying?” — is the enemy’s foundational challenge to faith. As a theme, this question echoes through Scripture as the adversary’s persistent attack on the ground of the believer’s confidence. Satan’s wilderness temptations of Jesus follow the same structure: “If you are the Son of God…” (Matthew 4:3,6) — a challenge to the ground of identity and trust. Christ answers not with Hezekiah’s political defense but with Scripture alone.
11: The accusation that Hezekiah is deceiving the people into death by claiming YHWH will deliver is an attempt to turn Judah’s trust in their king into the cause of their doom. As a theme, the enemy’s strategy of reframing trust in God’s anointed as a death trap anticipates the mockery at the cross: “He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him” (Matthew 27:43). The taunt is structurally identical — both target the relationship between God and his anointed.
12: Sennacherib argues that Hezekiah insulted YHWH by removing his high places. As a theme, the accusation that the faithful king has actually offended God echoes the charge brought against Christ by the religious authorities: that his works were demonic (Matthew 12:24), his words blasphemous (Matthew 26:65). In both cases, the adversary attempts to weaponize religious tradition against YHWH’s genuine representative.
13: “Were the gods of those nations able to deliver their lands from me?” Sennacherib lists his conquests as proof that YHWH cannot resist him. As a theme, the claim that past conquests prove divine impotence is the adversary’s greatest error — it is precisely what YHWH refutes by raising Christ from the dead. Death, sin, and the law seemed to conquer — the cross looked like Sennacherib’s victory over another god. The resurrection is the divine answer that no one could predict from the record of prior human defeats.
14: “Who among all the gods of those nations was able to deliver?” The rhetorical question assumes YHWH is merely one contestant among many. As a revelation of God, this verse sets up the great prophetic counter-claim of Isaiah 40-48: YHWH is not one deity among many but the creator of all nations, before whom the other gods are “nothing” (Isaiah 41:24). Christ embodies this divine singularity: “There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved” (Acts 4:12).
15: “Do not let Hezekiah deceive or mislead you.” The enemy’s final appeal is to suspicion of the anointed king himself. As a theme, the charge that the messianic figure is a deceiver runs from Sennacherib through Jeremiah’s accusers (“this man is deceiving us,” Jeremiah 38:4), to the charge against Jesus (“he is leading the people astray,” John 7:12), to the prediction that false christs will deceive many (Matthew 24:24). The genuine Christ endures the accusation of deception because he alone speaks the truth.
16: The servants compound the blasphemy against both YHWH and Hezekiah. As a theme, the institutional reinforcement of blasphemy — officials multiplying the accusation of their master — anticipates the institutional rejection of Christ: the chief priests, scribes, and elders all joining to condemn (Matthew 26:57-68). The corporate rejection of God’s anointed follows this pattern of escalating institutional condemnation.
17: The written letters equating YHWH with the gods of the nations are the most formal expression of the blasphemy. As a theme, the claim that YHWH is no different from defeated gods is what the NT calls the ultimate lie — the satanic inversion that presents the God of resurrection as just one power among many that can be defeated. Christ’s resurrection permanently refutes this: “He has been declared to be the Son of God in power by his resurrection from the dead” (Romans 1:4).
18: The Judean-language propaganda to demoralize the defenders. As a theme, the enemy speaking in the language of the covenant people to undermine their faith is a recurring strategy. The NT equivalent is false teaching that uses biblical language to undermine biblical faith: “false prophets who come to you in sheep’s clothing” (Matthew 7:15). Christ warns his own to test the spirits (1 John 4:1).
19: “They spoke against the God of Jerusalem as they would speak against the gods of the peoples of the earth, which are the work of human hands.” The equation of YHWH with manufactured gods is the foundational error. As a revelation of God, the distinction between YHWH and “the work of human hands” is absolute: YHWH is not made; he is the Maker. Christ embodies this distinction: “All things were made through him, and without him was not anything made that was made” (John 1:3). No human-made god can save; only the creator-God can.
20: “King Hezekiah and the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz prayed and cried out to heaven.” The royal-prophetic prayer partnership at the moment of greatest threat is the decisive act. As a type, the king and prophet together appealing to YHWH prefigures Christ’s own high-priestly prayer in John 17 — the Son appealing to the Father on behalf of his people, specifically at the moment of maximum opposition. Prayer is the act by which the covenant is activated in the crisis.
21: “The LORD sent an angel who struck down every mighty warrior, commander, and officer in the camp of the king of Assyria.” As a type, the divine warrior acting through an angelic agent to destroy the enemy of God’s anointed prefigures the eschatological judgment: at the parousia, Christ returns with the armies of heaven (Revelation 19:14) and his enemies are struck down. Sennacherib’s death “by the sword of his own sons” in the house of his own god is the ironic reversal: destroyed in the very sanctuary he thought would protect him.
22: “The LORD saved Hezekiah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem from the hand of Sennacherib.” The triple statement of YHWH’s saving act points to the comprehensive scope of divine deliverance. As a revelation of God, this verse establishes the character of YHWH as Savior — the attribute that reaches its fullest expression in the name of Jesus: Yēšûaʿ (YHWH saves). The deliverance of Jerusalem from Sennacherib is a temporal instance of the eternal salvation Christ accomplishes.
23: The nations bringing gifts to the LORD and to Hezekiah after the deliverance. As a theme, the Gentile tribute following divine victory over the enemy of God’s anointed is the recurring pattern: Solomon received tribute (2 Chr 9), the Magi bring gifts to the infant Christ (Matthew 2:1-12), and ultimately the kings of the earth bring their glory into the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:24-26). Each instance is a partial fulfillment of the Psalmic vision: “All kings shall fall down before him; all nations shall serve him” (Psalms 72:11).
24: Hezekiah’s mortal illness and his prayer. As a type, the faithful king facing death and crying out to God in prayer — and receiving a sign of extended life — is the pattern of Psalm 22 (the royal lament from the depths that ends in deliverance and universal praise). More directly, it anticipates Christ’s Gethsemane prayer — “he offered up prayers and supplications, with loud crying and tears, to him who was able to save him from death” (Hebrews 5:7). The difference: Christ’s prayer did not result in extended life but in resurrection through death.
25: “Hezekiah did not respond in proportion to the benefit he had received, for his heart was proud.” The failure of gratitude after grace — pride following blessing — is the recurring human failure. As a revelation of God, Hezekiah’s pride-after-deliverance is the very thing Christ comes to address: the human tendency to receive God’s gifts and turn inward with self-congratulation. Christ himself is the anti-type of this pride: “He humbled himself and became obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross” (Philippians 2:8).
26: “Hezekiah humbled himself (wayyikkānaʿ) for the pride of his heart… so the LORD’s wrath did not come upon them in the days of Hezekiah.” The humility that averts divine wrath is the OT instance of the principle Paul articulates in connection with Christ: “God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble” (James 4:6; 1 Peter 5:5, quoting Proverbs 3:34). Christ perfectly embodies the humility that averts wrath not just for one individual but for all: his self-humbling at the cross absorbs the wrath that the proud cannot escape.
27: Hezekiah’s great wealth and honor. As a theme, the covenantal blessing of wealth and honor given to the faithful king is the temporal shadow of the eschatological inheritance of Christ: “He is the heir of all things” (Hebrews 1:2), and those united to him share in his inheritance (Romans 8:17). The treasure-building of earthly kings points to the one who says, “Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth… lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven” (Matthew 6:19-20).
28: Storehouses for grain, wine, and oil; stalls for livestock. As a theme, the provision of the faithful king for his people’s food and livelihood echoes the motif of the divine shepherd who provides pasture (Psalms 23:2). Christ fulfills this as the one who feeds the five thousand (John 6:1-13) and who identifies himself as the bread of life (John 6:35): the true storehouse of sustenance for God’s people.
29: “God had given him great possessions.” The explicit attribution of wealth to divine gift rather than human achievement establishes the theology of stewardship that runs through both Testaments. As a revelation of God, every good gift comes from the Father of lights (James 1:17), and Christ is the one in whom “all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden” (Colossians 2:3): the ultimate gift given by the Father for the world’s blessing.
30: Hezekiah’s engineering of the Siloam Tunnel — bringing water into the city of David. As a shadow, the provision of water within the city anticipates Christ as the source of living water within the new Jerusalem: “The angel showed me the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb” (Revelation 22:1). Hezekiah channels physical water into Jerusalem; Christ channels the living water of the Spirit into the city of God forever.
31: “God left him to himself, in order to test him and to know all that was in his heart.” The divine testing of the faithful king’s heart is a motif that runs from Abraham (Genesis 22:1: “God tested Abraham”) through Moses to the wilderness testing of Israel (Deuteronomy 8:2: “that he might know what was in your heart”). The difference with Christ: tested in every respect as we are, yet without sin (Hebrews 4:15). Hezekiah failed the test with the Babylonian envoys; Christ passed every test, including Gethsemane.
32: The reference to the “vision of Isaiah the prophet” as a source document connects Hezekiah’s reign to the prophetic tradition that Christ fulfills. Isaiah’s “vision” (Isaiah 1:1) encompasses all 66 chapters, including the Servant Songs and the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53. The Chronicler’s citation of Isaiah as the interpretive source for Hezekiah’s reign places Hezekiah within the prophetic trajectory that ends in Christ.
33: Hezekiah is buried with honor in the upper tombs of David’s sons, and “all Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem honored him at his death.” As a theme, the honored burial of the faithful Davidic king is the OT anticipation of the resurrection and enthronement of Christ: laid in a tomb, yes, but not with the dishonor of the wicked (Isaiah 53:9: “he was with a rich man in his death”). The body of the anointed king matters; his death is not the final word.
